data.title: Waystar signals 2026 revenue of $1.274B-$1.294B while shifting Q2 sequential growth to flat-1%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Waystar Holding Corp. (WAY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Waystar delivered a solid start to the year reflecting strong execution across the business and our innovation road map as we continue to position ourselves as the market leader in delivering an end-to-end health care revenue cycle platform." (CEO & Director Matthew Hawkins)
  • "The AI era is expanding Waystar's total addressable market opportunity meaningfully." (CEO & Director Hawkins) "Historically, revenue cycle technology addressed a roughly $20 billion software market." (CEO & Director Hawkins) "That shift unlocks a much larger opportunity, the approximately $100 billion in annual revenue cycle labor services performed across the industry today." (CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "AI-powered capabilities drove roughly 40% of new bookings in Q1" (CEO & Director Hawkins) and "we continue to see RFP activity shift toward platform evaluations over point solutions." (CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "Revenue increased 22% year-over-year in the first quarter to $314 million." (Chief Financial Officer Steven Oreskovich) "Subscription revenue of $172 million for the first quarter increased 38% year-over-year" (Chief Financial Officer Oreskovich) and "volume-based revenue of $139 million for the first quarter increased 7% year-over-year." (Chief Financial Officer Oreskovich)

Outlook

  • "We reaffirm our revenue guidance range of $1.274 billion to $1.294 billion" and "adjusted EBITDA range of $530 million to $540 million." (Chief Financial Officer Oreskovich)
  • "While we previously communicated that we expect 1% to 3% sequential quarterly growth throughout 2026, with Q3 at the low end -- we now anticipate Q2 sequential growth to be flat to 1% and Q3 to be 1% to 3%." (Chief Financial Officer Oreskovich)

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted EBITDA of $135 million for the first quarter increased 26% year-over-year" and "the adjusted EBITDA margin of 43% was primarily driven by a shift to higher-margin solutions." (Chief Financial Officer Oreskovich)
  • "Our capital position is strong" with "$159 million in cash, equivalents and short-term investments" and "$1.5 billion in gross debt." (Chief Financial Officer Oreskovich)
  • "Unlevered free cash flow was $90 million" and "as of March 31, net leverage was 2.7x compared to 3x at the end of 2025." (Chief Financial Officer Oreskovich)

Q&A

  • Adam Hotchkiss, Goldman Sachs: "how should we think about the degree to which AI can be additive to your TAM and show up as accelerating revenue growth"; CEO & Director Hawkins: "It's going to allow Waystar to pursue a much larger addressable market opportunity" and "we view AI as a tailwind and as the biggest opportunity in our lifetime."
  • Charles Rhyee, TD Cowen: asked why "accelerated move from print to digital billing" impacts results and about "flat revenue" shaping; CFO Oreskovich: "that conversion is neutral" for "margin dollars or a cash flow" and "its going to allow us to see margin accretion in the business overall."
  • Jailendra Singh, Truist: asked about the "remaining 60% bookings" and conversion timing; CEO & Director Hawkins: "the bookings momentum is across both new prospects as well as cross-sell and upsell" and "they're still taking 6 to 18 months to show up in our revenue model."
  • Ryan Daniels, William Blair: asked how fast the new AI recruitment SKU can scale; CEO & Director Hawkins: "We do have a robust early adopter program" and "Depending on the SKU we're able to turn some of these things on rapidly."
  • Stanislav Berenshteyn, Wells Fargo: asked whether broader SKUs and larger sales are impacting sales cycles; CEO & Director Hawkins: "we're not noticing a compression of time or any elongation of time" and "we are also seeing elevated win rates."
  • Elizabeth Anderson, Evercore ISI: asked about hospital financing stress and payer pushback; CEO & Director Hawkins: "We're seeing our solution which tend to get prioritized" and "we'd like to think that Waystar's role can be that constructive referee that intermediary that brings fairness and transparency to the marketplace."
  • Constantine Davides, Citizens JMP: asked about acute/inpatient momentum; CEO & Director Hawkins: "approximately now we work with 16 of the top 20 hospitals and health systems in the United States" and "about 40% of our revenue today is hospital and health system or acute related."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were neutral-to-slightly negative in tone on near-term volume/shaping, pressing on mechanics and durability, including questions on "flat revenue" and "slowdown" tied to patient payments.
  • Management tone was slightly positive and explanatory, repeatedly emphasizing non-competitive drivers, including "none of these factors were competitive or product-driven" (Chief Financial Officer Oreskovich) and "this offset... has 0 to do with AI competition" (CEO & Director Hawkins).
  • Versus Q4 2025, management maintained high confidence in platform/AI positioning, while shifting to more caution on near-term patient payment seasonality, highlighted by "we now anticipate Q2 sequential growth to be flat to 1%." (Chief Financial Officer Oreskovich)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 maintained the full-year 2026 revenue and adjusted EBITDA ranges, while changing the sequential-growth shaping from "1% to 3% sequential quarterly growth throughout 2026" (Q4 2025) to "Q2 sequential growth to be flat to 1%" (Q1 2026). (Chief Financial Officer Oreskovich)
  • Management’s AI narrative broadened from Q4’s focus on outcomes like "prevented more than $15 billion in denials" (CEO & Director Hawkins, Q4 2025) to Q1’s emphasis on AI expanding the opportunity from "$20 billion" software toward the "$100 billion" labor pool. (CEO & Director Hawkins, Q1 2026)
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from Q4’s heavier emphasis on AI adoption and competitive landscape to Q1’s heavier concentration on patient payment volume drivers and the print-to-digital impact.

Risks and Concerns

  • "A few factors pressured patient payment volumes during the quarter" (CEO & Director Hawkins), and management attributed it to "accelerated conversion from print to digital patient statements," "changes in health care coverage" and "weather-related impacts." (Chief Financial Officer Oreskovich)
  • Management mitigation centered on business mix and pipeline visibility, including "provider solutions... comprised approximately 75% of revenue" (Chief Financial Officer Oreskovich) and "we carry what we believe is the largest qualified sales pipeline in our history... supporting visibility into 2027." (CEO & Director Hawkins)

Final Takeaway

Waystar’s management framed Q1 around continued platform execution and accelerating AI commercialization, while reaffirming full-year 2026 revenue and adjusted EBITDA ranges. They highlighted that patient payment volumes faced print-to-digital conversion and utilization-related offsets, prompting an updated expectation for Q2 sequential growth to be flat to 1%, while emphasizing that bookings, pipeline, and AI-driven product expansion remain central to longer-term growth and margin profile.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nvidia B300 servers hit $1M price in China amid U.S. curbs - report
data.description:
The price for Nvidia's (NVDA) B300 servers, key for AI tasks, is now around $1M (7M yuan) each in China due to surging demand and tightened U.S. export curbs that have disrupted supply chains, Reuters reported, citing industry sources.

The B300 server—the chipmaker's most powerful system for AI workloads—scaled sharply from around 4M yuan late last year, reflecting a scarcity premium driven by stricter U.S. export curbs. The price rose sharply after the grey market, a key supply channel, came under pressure, the sources said.

In the U.S., a B300 server, which houses eight B300 GPUs, costs about $550,000, up slightly from around $500,000 earlier, highlighting the growing price gap due to limited supply in China.

The shortage comes after U.S. authorities in March prosecuted Yih-Shyan "Wally" Liaw, a co-founder of Nvidia partner Supermicro, the sources added.

Nvidia (NVDA) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Some companies, priced out by the surge, are turning to rentals instead, with one-year short-term contracts costing as much as 190,000 yuan per month.

data.title: RRE Ventures Acquisition prices $250M IPO at $10 per share
data.description:
• RRE Ventures Acquisition (RREVU) priced its IPO at $250M, consisting of 25M units priced at $10.00 per unit.
  • The underwriters have a 45-day option to purchase up to an additional 3.75M units at the IPO price to cover over-allotments.
  • The Class A ordinary shares and warrants are expected to be listed on Nasdaq under the symbols “RREV” and “RREVW,” respectively.
  • The offering is expected to close on May 1, 2026.

data.title: BBVA Compass Bancshares Non-GAAP EPS of €0.51, revenue of €7.54B
data.description:
• BBVA Compass Bancshares press release (BBVA): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of €0.51.
  • Revenue of €7.54B (+17.8% Y/Y).
  • The BBVA Group's CET1 ratio stood at 12.83% as of March 31, 2026.

data.title: Avalyn Pharma prices $300M IPO at $18 per share
data.description:
• Clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company Avalyn Pharma (AVLN) priced its upsized IPO of 16.67M shares at a price to the public of $18.00 per share.
  • The gross proceeds are expected to be $300M.
  • The underwriters have a 30-day option to buy an additional 2.5M shares at the initial public offering price.
  • The shares are expected to begin trading on the Nasdaq on April 30, 2026, under the ticker symbol “AVLN.”
  • The offering is expected to close on May 1, 2026.
  • The company is focused on developing drugs for serious lung diseases, especially pulmonary fibrosis and other rare respiratory conditions.

data.title: Viking outlines 52-week VANQUISH extension and plans oral VK2735 Phase III start in Q4 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Viking Therapeutics (VKTX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Brian Lian said the quarter’s operational focus was advancing VK2735 across multiple Phase III-enabling paths, stating, "we made significant progress with our obesity franchise, highlighted by our lead compound, VK2735" and adding that "we were pleased to announce the completion of enrollment in the VANQUISH-2 trial. Both studies continue to proceed on track."
  • Lian highlighted a key development change for the oral program, saying, "based on the positive top line results reported in our Phase II VENTURE-Oral dosing study, the company elected to advance oral VK2735 into Phase III clinical development" and specifying, "We currently expect to initiate this program in the fourth quarter of this year."
  • Lian emphasized optionality and administration upgrades inside Phase III, stating, "participants in both VANQUISH studies began transitioning to the auto-injector device" and adding, "This transition has been proceeding smoothly."
  • Gregory Zante quantified the step-up in investment, saying, "Research and development expenses were $150.2 million for the 3 months ended March 31, 2026" and "General and administrative expenses were $14 million."
  • Zante summarized profitability and liquidity, stating, "For the 3 months ended March 31, 2026, Viking recorded a net loss of $158.3 million or $1.37 per share" and "At March 31, 2026, Viking held cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of $603 million."
  • Lian framed the commercialization build-out around a new executive hire, stating, "the company announced in the first quarter the appointment of Neil Aubuchon as its first Chief Commercial Officer" and adding, "We are excited to have him on board to lead this critical operation."

Outlook

  • Lian guided to multiple clinical catalysts, saying, "We expect to report the results of this study in the third quarter" (maintenance dosing study) and "Pending clearance, we expect to initiate a Phase I clinical trial for VK3019 later this quarter."
  • On the oral Phase III timing, Lian said, "We currently expect to initiate this program in the fourth quarter of this year," and in Q&A clarified the shift in start timing by stating, "Nothing really changed" and attributing the push to scale-up realities: "making 100 tablets is different than making 1 million tablets."
  • On how maintenance data could feed Phase III extensions, Lian said, "we believe the results from these maintenance regimens could be utilized in the upcoming 52-week VANQUISH extension studies" and later confirmed, "we plan to run the extension for 52 weeks."

Financial Results

  • Zante attributed the widened loss primarily to development acceleration, saying, "The increase in net loss for the 3 months ended March 31, 2026, was primarily due to increased research and development expenses."
  • Zante described the period cash decline as balance-sheet funded execution, stating, "At March 31, 2026, Viking held cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of $603 million compared to $706 million as of December 31, 2025."

Q&A

  • Steven Seedhouse, Cantor Fitzgerald & Co.: Asked for the added maintenance cohorts and whether R&D would fall; President, CEO & Director Brian Lian detailed, "we'll retain those first 4 cohorts...22.5, 20, 17.5 mg monthly as well as 7.5 mg every other week" and added new arms: "15 monthly, 10 monthly, 10 every other week and 5 every other week"; CFO Gregory Zante said, "the disconnect...is really timing" and added, "toward the second half of this year, I would expect things to taper down a little bit" and "we would anticipate having cash into '28."
  • Nathanael Charoensook, Leerink Partners: Asked VANQUISH baselines and expected T2D vs non-T2D weight loss; Lian said, "we're actually going to present the baseline demographics at 2 conferences" and on efficacy expectations added, "type 2 patients are a little bit more resistant to weight loss than non-type 2 patients."
  • Michael Ulz, Morgan Stanley: Asked which maintenance option resonates with patients; Lian responded, "we're not really getting that level of feedback" and emphasized study integrity: "it would be hard to interpret because it's a placebo-controlled study."
  • Ryan Deschner, Raymond James: Asked why 19-week induction and whether oral cohorts could expand; Lian said induction was "driven by the time to titrate to the 22.5 mg dose" and confirmed, "we will be adding more cohorts to the oral portion of the study."
  • Annabel Samimy, Stifel: Pressed how cohorts translate to VANQUISH extensions and labeling; Lian said, "there will be more than 1 dosing arm in the extension studies" and confirmed, "It will be multiple maintenance cohorts there that will be drawn from these data."
  • Biren Amin, Piper Sandler: Asked when maintenance begins in extensions and transition issues; Lian said, "we'll transition people at 78 weeks" and "we plan to run the extension for 52 weeks" and noted potential tolerability questions: "there could be some incremental adverse events...but those would all be things that we'll find out during the trial."
  • Jay Olson, Oppenheimer: Asked if maintenance readout gated oral Phase III and plans for VK3019; Lian said, "they're just independent factors" and for VK3019 outlined, "the first 2 studies will be a SAD study and then a MAD study" while adding, "We are initiating combo tox with the 2735 compound."
  • Tsan-Yu Hsieh, William Blair: Asked about extension flexibility; Lian said, "It's not really an open-label study" and added, "we probably won't discuss too many details until we actually start it."
  • Hardik Parikh, JPMorgan: Asked tablet burden and oral-market takeaways; Lian said, "we would want to have no more than 2 tablets" and Chief Commercial Officer Neil Aubuchon said, "what we're seeing is this is growing the market" and added, "we expect to have the first dual agonist oral on the market."
  • Yale Jen, Laidlaw: Asked if VANQUISH extensions include T2D maintenance; Lian answered, "Yes, it will. Yes."
  • Blake Gitler, BTIG: Asked what “good” looks like in maintenance data; Lian said, "best case scenario is you see a continuation of weight loss" and added, "our base case is...a real maintenance, less than really a few percent either way up or down."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, with repeated probing on cost trajectory and study design specificity, including Seedhouse flagging expectations that "R&D [was] to come down" and multiple analysts pressing for regimen selection mechanics.
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded in protocol detail during Q&A, including Lian saying, "we probably won't discuss too many details" while still projecting confidence on execution timelines such as "Both studies continue to proceed on track."
  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call featured more detailed regimen granularity (expanded cohorts and explicit VANQUISH extension length) and more frequent boundary-setting on disclosures, particularly around baseline characteristics and extension design specifics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Oral Phase III timing language shifted from Q3 (prior call: "We currently expect to initiate this program in the third quarter of this year") to Q4 (current call: "We currently expect to initiate this program in the fourth quarter of this year"), with Lian attributing it to manufacturing scale-up learning.
  • The auto-injector moved from planned introduction (prior call: "We expect this transition to occur later this quarter") to in-trial implementation (current call: "participants in both VANQUISH studies began transitioning to the auto-injector device").
  • The maintenance study broadened from an initial framework to a larger subcutaneous exploration, with Lian describing an expansion "from 4 to 8" subcutaneous dosing arms to inform VANQUISH extensions.
  • Financially, quarterly R&D remained at a similar magnitude across the two most recent quarters (current: $150.2 million; prior: $153.5 million), while cash balance declined from $706 million at year-end to $603 million at Q1 end, alongside management reiterating runway expectations.

Risks and Concerns

  • Zante flagged near-term expense levels as influenced by timing, saying, "the disconnect...is really timing" and guided variability by adding that spending could be "around where we were at quarter 1, maybe a bit lower" before "taper down" later in the year.
  • Lian emphasized operational complexity risks tied to scaling the oral program, stating, "making 100 tablets is different than making 1 million tablets," and positioned process optimization as the reason for the Q4 Phase III start.
  • Multiple analysts focused on maintenance-dose selection and extension execution risk; Lian’s mitigation was to broaden the dataset, saying the expanded cohorts will provide "a broad and robust data set" for extension-arm selection.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as an execution-heavy quarter centered on VK2735, marked by completed VANQUISH-2 enrollment, in-trial transition to an auto-injector, an expanded eight-arm subcutaneous maintenance study with data expected in Q3, and a revised expectation to initiate oral VK2735 Phase III in Q4 2026 due to scale-up and manufacturing process work, alongside plans to start VK3019 Phase I after IND clearance and to run a 52-week VANQUISH extension with multiple maintenance cohorts.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: L3Harris files confidentially for IPO of its missile business
data.description:
• Defense supplier L3Harris (LHX) on Wednesday said that it has confidentially ‌submitted a draft registration statement for ‌the initial public offering of its missile solutions business.
  • The ​company said the number of shares to be offered and the price range for the proposed IPO are yet to ‌be determined.
  • In January, L3Harris ⁠said it plans to sell new equity in its growing rocket ⁠motor business, creating a new company backed by a $1B U.S. government convertible ​security investment.
  • The stock has risen about 10% YTD.

data.title: Billionaire investor Barry Sternlicht’s Starwood Capital halts redemptions from REIT
data.description:
Billionaire investor Barry Sternlicht’s Starwood Capital Group has halted redemptions from its $22B Starwood Real Estate Income Trust ((SREIT)), underscoring ongoing stress in private real estate vehicles amid weak commercial property conditions.

The firm said it is “temporarily suspending” share repurchases following a strategic review, aiming to preserve liquidity while waiting for a recovery in commercial real estate markets. The move also includes a cut in distributions, with the annualized payout reduced to about 4.7% from 6.3% for Class I share.

"the issue we are addressing is not the real estate." a shareholder update read. "It is the pressure created by elevated redemption requests, which rose quite suddenly when interest rates spiked and have remained high."

The suspension is effective April 29, 2026, and applies to April share repurchase requests, exempting small accounts under $5,000 and cases of death or disability. 

Sternlicht noted in a shareholder letter that ongoing redemption pressure is not expected to persist, with plans to restore liquidity sustainably as revenue growth returns.

SREIT, launched in 2018, holds 598 income-producing properties worth $22.4B at 94% occupancy as of March 31, 2026. Starwood Capital has invested $500M, owning about 7% alongside affiliates.

data.title: France inflation expected to rise to 2.2%, GDP growth stalled
data.description:
France’s consumer price inflation accelerated to 2.2% Y/Y in April 2026, up from 1.7% in March and surpassing market forecasts of 2.0%, according to a preliminary estimate.

The CPI climbed 1.0% M/M in April 2026, the same pace as in March and slightly above market expectations of a 0.9% increase.

France’s economy stalled quarter-on-quarter in Q1 2026, compared with market expectations and the previous period’s 0.2% growth, preliminary estimates showed. 

On an annual basis, GDP expanded 1.1%, following a 1.3% increase in Q4.

data.title: McGrath projects 2026 revenue of $945M-$995M while keeping adjusted EBITDA outlook of $360M-$378M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: McGrath RentCorp (MGRC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Total company revenues increased 2% and adjusted EBITDA decreased 1% compared to the prior year first quarter." (President, CEO & Director Philip Hawkins)
  • "We delivered rental revenue growth in each of our businesses despite some challenging market conditions." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "At Mobile Modular, rental revenues grew 4%." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) "Our commercial market segments were the primary drivers of our growth." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "Our Architecture Billings Index, or ABI, and other macro indicators of construction-related demand remained subdued." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) "Despite this, our quote and booking levels were higher than a year ago with our geographic expansion efforts and additional sales coverage contributing to these positive trends." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "At TRS, rental revenues continued their recent growth trajectory and were up 13%." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) "Demand continued to be strong across the broad spectrum of our equipment, and we benefited from project supporting build-out of new data centers." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "We also worked on a small modular acquisition, which we closed in early April." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) "In addition, we completed share repurchases during the quarter." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "Total revenues increased 2% to $199 million, and adjusted EBITDA decreased 1% to $74 million." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Keith E. Pratt)

Outlook

  • "For the full year, our outlook remains unchanged, and we expect total revenue between $945 million and $995 million, adjusted EBITDA between $360 million and $378 million, and gross rental equipment capital expenditures between $180 million and $200 million." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • "Recent developments in the Middle East had no material impact in the first quarter." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) "This could change as the year progresses and may increase uncertainty or result in customers delaying projects." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "Additionally, higher energy prices for an extended period may start to impact operating costs." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)

Financial Results

  • "Total revenues increased 2% to $199 million, and adjusted EBITDA decreased 1% to $74 million." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • "Inventory center costs increased by $3.2 million as we prepared equipment to support higher shipment levels." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt) "This expense compressed rental margins to 56%, down from 60% a year ago." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • "TRS had a strong quarter. with total revenues up 11% to $39 million and adjusted EBITDA up 16% to $21 million." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt) "Rental margins improved to 45% from 40% a year ago." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • "Enviroplex total sales revenue decreased 51% to $3.7 million, and adjusted EBITDA declined to a loss of $1.1 million from a profit of $0.4 million." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • "Net cash provided by operating activities was $42 million compared to $54 million in the prior year." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt) "Rental equipment purchases were $45 million compared to $12 million in the prior year." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • "At quarter end, we had net borrowings of $546 million, and the ratio of funded debt to the last 12 months actual adjusted EBITDA was 1.51 to 1." (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)

Q&A

  • John Ronan Kennedy, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked which modular end markets are the largest contributors and whether education demand is changing; Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt said the work "would fall into what you would call the mega project category" and added that "our first quarter education bookings were roughly flat year-over-year."
  • John Ronan Kennedy, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked about post-quarter caution tied to the Middle East and where higher energy costs would hit; President, CEO & Director Hawkins said "We haven't seen any material impact" and added "the place that you'd probably see that occur first if this is extended, would be in the fuel cost area" while noting "the majority of those costs were able to pass through the customers"; Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt added "It's just way too early to get any read."
  • John Ronan Kennedy, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked why bookings improved while utilization declined; President, CEO & Director Hawkins said "there's some period of time it can be months before those project sites are ready" and Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt said "we're still getting returns that are higher than the ships."
  • Scott Schneeberger, Oppenheimer & Co. Inc., Research Division: asked why MGRC resumed buybacks and whether they continue; Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt said "share repurchases are something we review on a regular basis" and cited "over 1.8 million shares available and authorized for repurchase" under the plan.
  • Scott Schneeberger, Oppenheimer & Co. Inc., Research Division: asked about April trends and seasonality; President, CEO & Director Hawkins said April activity was "consistent with what we experienced in Q1" with "solid bookings in Mobile Modular, still kind of flattish in portable storage and continued strength of TRS."
  • Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.: asked about Mobile Modular price vs. volume; Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt said "we're getting 7% more revenue per unit" and "we had roughly 3% fewer units on rent."
  • Steven Ramsey, Thompson Research Group, LLC: asked whether TRS CapEx implies fleet expansion; Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt said "we are more than happy now to add capital" and added "one of the reasons we increased the gross CapEx guide for this year, is, in fact, we saw the probability that this good opportunity at TRS would continue"; President, CEO & Director Hawkins said "I'd pay more attention...to the actual dollars of CapEx and fleet size than units" due to cost variability.
  • Marc Riddick, Sidoti & Company, LLC: asked about competitive pressure in portable storage deliveries/pickups; Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt said "it's harder to cover your costs now than it was 2 or 3 years ago" and added "for us, getting to breakeven would be great."

Sentiment Analysis

  • "Can you kind of talk about how that reconciles internal strength versus weaker indicators?" (John Ronan Kennedy, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division) and "Are you seeing green shoots" (Dan Moore, CJS Securities, Inc.) reflected analyst focus on demand durability, utilization pressure, and competitive intensity; Scott Schneeberger’s tone was more upbeat, calling out, "Real nice quarter" and probing capital returns.
  • "We haven't seen any material impact" (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) and "It's just way too early to get any read" (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt) reflected a watchful tone on macro/geopolitical risks, while Hawkins also said "I'm confident we have the right team and discipline in place to drive shareholder value in the years ahead."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s prepared emphasis on leadership transition and 2026 setup, the current call’s tone centered more on execution progress, buybacks, and TRS/data center-driven demand; analyst questions similarly shifted from succession/initial guide framing to utilization, cost pressure, and buyback intent.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q1 2026, management reiterated: "For the full year, our outlook remains unchanged" with the same 2026 ranges previously issued. (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt)
  • The biggest narrative shift from Q4 2025 to Q1 2026 was leadership: Q4 noted, "It will be my final earnings call as CEO" and "Phil Hawkins is the best leader to succeed me" (President, CEO & Director Joseph Hanna), while Q1 was Hawkins’ first call as CEO.
  • Capital return emphasis increased in Q1, with management stating "we completed share repurchases during the quarter" (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) and Q&A focusing on the decision; in Q4, buybacks were not a featured topic.
  • TRS momentum remained a consistent theme across both calls, with Q1 reiterating project support for "build-out of new data centers" (President, CEO & Director Hawkins) and Q4 describing "nice demand from data centers" in communications fleet. (President, CEO & Director Hanna)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Our Architecture Billings Index, or ABI, and other macro indicators of construction-related demand remained subdued." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "Higher equipment preparation expenses" and "higher costs compressed profitability" were described as pressures in Mobile Modular and portable storage. (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • "Recent developments in the Middle East had no material impact in the first quarter" but "may increase uncertainty or result in customers delaying projects." (President, CEO & Director Hawkins)
  • In portable storage, management highlighted that delivery and pickup economics remain pressured, with Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt saying "we've seen some of the smaller players much more willing to sharpen their pencil" on pricing.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a steady start with "rental revenue growth in each of our divisions" (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Corporate Secretary Pratt), unchanged full-year guidance, and heightened investor attention on capital allocation after the company "completed share repurchases during the quarter" (President, CEO & Director Hawkins). The call emphasized TRS strength tied to data center-related project work, ongoing modular geographic expansion and services attach, and continued caution around subdued construction indicators, competitive portable storage conditions, and potential spillover risks from higher energy prices and geopolitical uncertainty.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
